{{ill|Q700448|lt=Q700448|WD=Q700448}} == image of entrance (P9721) == * Ota-jinja1.JPG {{commons category|Category:Ota-jinja (Kyoto)}} [[JA:大田神社 (京都市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11437955}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Ota Shrine |Image = [[File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) haiden.JPG|280px]]
House of worship |Location = {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kyoto City|Q34600}}{{jalink|Kita Ward (Kyoto City)|Q1154970|Kita Ward}}Kamigamo Motoyama 340 |Location = {{Coord|35|03|35.4|N|135|45|30.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ota Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Amenouzume|Q462803|Tenden Onme no Mikoto}} |Divinity = |Shaku = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
{{jalink|Kamobetsu Raijinja|Q700448}}Kyotogaisetsusha |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}}{{jalink|cypress bark roof|Q90373477}} |Alternative name = Ontasha |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{jalink|April 10th|Q2502}}・{{jalink|November 10th|Q2988}}{{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}} |Shinto ritual = }} [[File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) torii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Torii]]'''Ota Shrine'' (Ota Jinja) is {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} located in the Kamigamo main temple. {{jalink|Shikinai Shrine}}, currently outside the precincts of {{jalink|Kamobetsu Rai Shrine|Q700448}} (Kamigamo Shrine) {{jalink|Setsusha|Q135009904}} (Third Setsusha). In ancient times, it was also called ``Ontasha''. It is located approximately 500 meters east of Kamigamo Shrine. === Enshrined God === The following deities are enshrined: {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. * '''{{jalink|Ame no Uzume|Q462803|Ame no Uzume}}''' (Ameno Uzume Mikoto) It is said to be the oldest shrine in Kamo, and is believed to have longevity {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. === History === Founding date is unknown. It is said that it was worshiped by the indigenous people before {{jalink|Kamo Prefectural Lord|Q2975287|Kamo Prefectural Lord}} (Kamo Agatanushi) immigrated to this area, but it is not clear {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. {{jalink|extension (era name)|Q1327274|Extended}} In "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) nk|Yamashiro Province|Q749276}}{{jalink|Atago District|Q10981345}}, it is written as ``Ota Shrine'' (or ``Ota Shrine'' in some manuscripts), and is listed in {{jalink|Shikiuchi}} {{Sfn|Ota Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1979}}. === Precincts === === Shrine building === [[File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) honden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|main hall]] Both the main shrine and worship hall were rebuilt in {{jalink|Kanei|Q1192913}}5 ({{jalink|1628|Q6840}}) {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. The main shrine is an Ichigen shrine {{jalink|Nagazuzukuri|Q210684}}, and the roof is {{jalink|cypress bark |Q90373477}}{{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. The worship hall is an old style called a ``warihaiden'' (a worship hall with an atrium in the center that can be passed through), and the roof is made of cypress bark like the main shrine {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. In the company journal, it appears that the Ota oratory "toppled over" in March of {{jalink|Tenju (Japan)|Q1145023|Tenju}} 3rd year ({{jalink|1377|Q6289}}), and it appears that it was built before that {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. === Snake Pillow === [[Image:Ota-jinja3.JPG|thumb|150px|Snake pillow (lower center stone)]] A small stone peeks out from the water below the right side of the stone bridge in front of the torii gate. This stone is called ``''Snake's Pillow'' or ``Amaishi'' and is said to have been used as a pillow by a snake. Snakes were thought to be creatures that brought rain, and it was believed that if you went to the pillow where this snake was located, you could pray for rain. The ritual is performed by hitting this pillow stone with agricultural tools (iron tools). By doing this, the snake that is hit by the pillow gets angry and starts raining ``The Snake Pillow of Ota Shrine'' (Talk with Rocks 141) Kyoto Shimbun July 8, 1999. === Setsusha === * Shirahige Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{jalink|Sarutahiko no Mikoto}} * Hyakudaifusha - Deity enshrined: {{jalink|Funatama-kami|Q11614362|Funatama-kami}} (Funatama no Kami) * Chinjusha - Deities enshrined: {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōkuninushi no kami}} (Ookuninushi no kami), {{jalink|Sukunabikona|Q3275687|Sukunahiko no kami}} (Sukunahiko no kami) * Fukutokusha - Enshrined deity: Fukutokujin All of the above four shrines are positioned as outer shrines of Kamigamo Shrine {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=57-59}}. File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) Shirahige-sha.JPG|Shirahige-sha File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) Hyakudayu-sha.JPG|Hyakudayu-sha File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) Chinju-sha.JPG|Chinjusha === Festival === * Gonaigiki Gansai ({{jalink|January 10th|Q2243}}, {{jalink|May 10th|Q2557}}, {{jalink|September 10th|Q2853}}) * Spring Festival [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/event/index.html Kamigamo Shrine Festivals and Events] ({{jalink|April 10th|Q2502}}) ** Parishioners will be dedicating flower arrangements and praying throughout the day in the precincts. At the time of the prayer, ``Sato Kagura'' (Champon Kagura), a registered intangible folk cultural property of Kyoto City, is dedicated. Because Ota Shrine is a shrine with a long lifespan, it is characterized by the fact that it is performed by elderly people and has little movement. The name ``Champon Kagura'' was born from the sound. * Autumn Festival ({{jalink|November 10th|Q2988}}) ** Prayers and Kagura performances are accepted during the day, and a fire festival is held in the evening. A chrysanthemum exhibition will be held at the temple grounds throughout the day. === Kakitsubata community in Otanosawa === [[Image:Ota-jinja Kakitsubata.jpg|thumb|190px|Ota-jinja Kakitsubata community (national {{jalink|natural monument|Q23790}})]] At ``Otanozawa'' next to the approach, approximately 25,000 {{jalink|Kakitsubata|Q159320}} plants grow wild on a site of approximately 2,000 square meters, and it is called ``''Otanosawa's Kakitsubata colony''''. This Otanosawa has been a famous place since the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, and there is a legend that it was the motif of {{jalink|Korin Ogata|Q952938}}'s ``Kakitsubata'' [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/topics/2010/2010_kakitsubata.html Kamigamo Shrine 2010 Information on Ota Shrine "Kakitsubata"]. When it blooms from early to mid-May every year, it blooms in various shades of purple all over the stream, delighting the eyes of many tourists.Otanosawa is said to have been a swamp in ancient times, similar to {{jalink|Shindoike|Q11560138}}, and is said to be a vestige of the time when {{jalink|Kyoto Basin|Q10883263}} was once a lake. In 1939|Q18639}, it was designated as a national {{jalink|natural monument|Q23790}}, along with the Kakitsubata community. In {{jalink|Bunji|Q1194415}}6 ({{jalink|1190|Q19736}}), the famous editor of ``{{jalink|Senzai Wakashu|Q1143290}}'', {{jalink|Fujiwara Toshinari|Q934255}}, , he composes the following song, likening the scene of being dyed in purple to a simple love. ``Violet grass shines bright in May, best time to see it at Ota Shrine in Kita Ward'' Kyoto Shimbun, May 6, 2010]. {{Quotation|Kakitsubata of the Kamiyama (Koyama) and Ota's swamp The flowers gleam in color
''The meaning of the song: Is the love affair that is deeply requested to the Kakitsubata of Ota Shrine near Kamiyama (the place where Kamobetsu Rai no Mikoto descended) as earnest and beautiful as the color of the Kakitsubata? ''|{{jalink|Toshinari Fujiwara|Q934255}}}} === Legend of full pond and dry pond === It is said that when praying for rain, if you change the water in Ota-no-ike (another name for Ota-no-sawa), it will rain, and during long rains, if you change the water in Jinguji-no-ike (the pond that used to be part of Jingu-ji Temple at Kamigamo Shrine), the rain will stop. Likened to {{jalink|Manju Island/Kanju Island|Q11563922|Kanju/Manju}}, the pond in Ota was called Manchi, and the pond at Jinguji Temple was called Hanike.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url= https://rmda.kulib.kyoto-u.ac.jp/item/rb00006109#?c=0&m=0&s=0&cv=0&r=0&xywh=-1144%2C-115%2C5358%2C2275 |title=Kyoto University Digital Archive Rare Document Images - Kamo Famous Places Stories|year=2002 |accessdate=2023-10-04|publisher={{jalink|Kyoto University|Q336264|Kyoto University Library}}}}. In the past, {{jalink|Eight Great Dragon Kings|Q11390736}} was enshrined in the pond, and HachiikeA collective name for {{jalink|Kibune Shrine|Q276779|Kibune}} Ryuo Falls, Nonaka Shimizu, Funasashi, Jinguji Pond (dry pond), Ota Pond (full pond), Kurojaike Pond, Shinjaike Pond, and {{jalink|Shindoike Pond|Q11560138|Midoike}}, where the Eight Great Dragonlords were solicited. (From "Kamo Meisho Monogatari" held by Kyoto University Library) was counted as one of the === Ota's path === At the rear of the shrine grounds, there is a walking path with a total length of approximately 750 meters called ``Ota no Komichi''. This walking path was developed in {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} in 2017 ({{jalink|2005|Q2019}}) by the Kamigamo Neighborhood Association, the Kamigamo Town Development Committee, and other members of local residents. {{PDFlink|[https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/sogo/cmsfiles/contents/0000035/35299/sono3-3-2.pdf Community Activities Support Guide Efforts of Kita Ward Kamigamo School District Community development with local "pride"]}}.There is an unpaved but well-maintained mountain path that runs up the small mountain behind the shrine. There are {{jalink|ropes|Q31029}} on both sides of the walking path, and there are {{jalink|signposts|Q1211272}} at several places along the way, so you can stroll without getting lost. {{jalink|Azalea|Azalea|Q189393|Mountain azalea}}, which is said to have been loved by Kitaoji Rosanjin|Q7367324}}, greets you as you walk along the mountain path. On good days, you can see {{jalink|Kyoto Tower|Q1201304}} and {{jalink|Fushimi Castle|Q1196273|Fushimi Momoyama Castle}}. By going through the cedar forest and following the walking path, you will reach the Okamoto Exit (east side climbing entrance, Kamigamo Okahoncho area) [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/kita/page/0000056124.html Kyoto City Information Center Kita Ward Office Our Town Kita Ward - Kamigamo School District News][https://www.city.kyoto.jp/kita/map/spot2.html#oota_komiti Kita Ward Spot Guide - Kyoto City Kita Ward Walking Map Ota Path]. The guide board for ``Ota no Komichi'' is written by the chief priest of Kamigamo Shrine on the Ota Shrine side and by the Mayor of Kyoto City's Kita Ward on the Okamoto Exit side. Image:Otanokomichi1.JPG|Entrance to Ota Shrine side Image:Otanokomichi2.JPG|Ota's small mountain path Image:Otanokomichi3.JPG|The view from the observation pointImage:Otanokomichi4.JPG|Okamoto exit side entrance === Cultural assets === === National Natural Monument === * Otanosawa's Kakitsubata community - Designated on September 7, 1945 {{Nationally designated cultural properties database|401|1694|Otanosawa's Kakitsubata community}}. === Kyoto City Registered Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Ota Shrine Shrine Goddess Music - Registered on May 1, 1986 [http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005866.html Kyoto City Designated and Registered Cultural Properties - Intangible Folk Cultural Properties - Kita Ward] (Kyoto City website "Kyoto City Information Center"). . === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group=†}} === Source === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|Japanese Izumitani|chapter=Ota Shrine|year=1979|title=Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report Volume 1|publisher={{jalink|Kogakkan University|Q1797584}}Publishing Department|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Ota Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1979}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Mitsugi Kennai|chapter=The history of Setsusesha|year=2003|title=Kamigamo Shrine|publisher={{jalink|Kamikamo Shrine|Q11448819}}|isbn=4311407203|ref={{Harvid|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Company)|2003}}}} === Related items === {{Commonscat|Ota-jinja (Kyoto)}} * {{jalink|Kamobetsu Rai Shrine|Q700448}} * {{ill|Q700448|lt=Q700448|WD=Q700448}} == image of entrance (P9721) == * Ota-jinja1.JPG {{commons category|Category:Ota-jinja (Kyoto)}} [[JA:大田神社 (京都市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11437955}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Ota Shrine |Image = [[File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) haiden.JPG|280px]]
House of worship |Location = {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kyoto City|Q34600}}{{jalink|Kita Ward (Kyoto City)|Q1154970|Kita Ward}}Kamigamo Motoyama 340 |Location = {{Coord|35|03|35.4|N|135|45|30.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ota Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Amenouzume|Q462803|Tenden Onme no Mikoto}} |Divinity = |Shaku = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small)
{{jalink|Kamobetsu Raijinja|Q700448}}Kyotogaisetsusha |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{jalink|Ryuzo|Q210684}}{{jalink|cypress bark roof|Q90373477}} |Alternative name = Ontasha |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{jalink|April 10th|Q2502}}・{{jalink|November 10th|Q2988}}{{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}} |Shinto ritual = }} [[File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) torii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Torii]]'''Ota Shrine'' (Ota Jinja) is {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} located in the Kamigamo main temple. {{jalink|Shikinai Shrine}}, currently outside the precincts of {{jalink|Kamobetsu Rai Shrine|Q700448}} (Kamigamo Shrine) {{jalink|Setsusha|Q135009904}} (Third Setsusha). In ancient times, it was also called ``Ontasha''. It is located approximately 500 meters east of Kamigamo Shrine. === Enshrined God === The following deities are enshrined: {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. * '''{{jalink|Ame no Uzume|Q462803|Ame no Uzume}}''' (Ameno Uzume Mikoto) It is said to be the oldest shrine in Kamo, and is believed to have longevity {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. === History === Founding date is unknown. It is said that it was worshiped by the indigenous people before {{jalink|Kamo Prefectural Lord|Q2975287|Kamo Prefectural Lord}} (Kamo Agatanushi) immigrated to this area, but it is not clear {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. {{jalink|extension (era name)|Q1327274|Extended}} In "{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}) nk|Yamashiro Province|Q749276}}{{jalink|Atago District|Q10981345}}, it is written as ``Ota Shrine'' (or ``Ota Shrine'' in some manuscripts), and is listed in {{jalink|Shikiuchi}} {{Sfn|Ota Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1979}}. === Precincts === === Shrine building === [[File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) honden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|main hall]] Both the main shrine and worship hall were rebuilt in {{jalink|Kanei|Q1192913}}5 ({{jalink|1628|Q6840}}) {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. The main shrine is an Ichigen shrine {{jalink|Nagazuzukuri|Q210684}}, and the roof is {{jalink|cypress bark |Q90373477}}{{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. The worship hall is an old style called a ``warihaiden'' (a worship hall with an atrium in the center that can be passed through), and the roof is made of cypress bark like the main shrine {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. In the company journal, it appears that the Ota oratory "toppled over" in March of {{jalink|Tenju (Japan)|Q1145023|Tenju}} 3rd year ({{jalink|1377|Q6289}}), and it appears that it was built before that {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. === Snake Pillow === [[Image:Ota-jinja3.JPG|thumb|150px|Snake pillow (lower center stone)]] A small stone peeks out from the water below the right side of the stone bridge in front of the torii gate. This stone is called ``''Snake's Pillow'' or ``Amaishi'' and is said to have been used as a pillow by a snake. Snakes were thought to be creatures that brought rain, and it was believed that if you went to the pillow where this snake was located, you could pray for rain. The ritual is performed by hitting this pillow stone with agricultural tools (iron tools). By doing this, the snake that is hit by the pillow gets angry and starts raining ``The Snake Pillow of Ota Shrine'' (Talk with Rocks 141) Kyoto Shimbun July 8, 1999. === Setsusha === * Shirahige Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{jalink|Sarutahiko no Mikoto}} * Hyakudaifusha - Deity enshrined: {{jalink|Funatama-kami|Q11614362|Funatama-kami}} (Funatama no Kami) * Chinjusha - Deities enshrined: {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōkuninushi no kami}} (Ookuninushi no kami), {{jalink|Sukunabikona|Q3275687|Sukunahiko no kami}} (Sukunahiko no kami) * Fukutokusha - Enshrined deity: Fukutokujin All of the above four shrines are positioned as outer shrines of Kamigamo Shrine {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=57-59}}. File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) Shirahige-sha.JPG|Shirahige-sha File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) Hyakudayu-sha.JPG|Hyakudayu-sha File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) Chinju-sha.JPG|Chinjusha === Festival === * Gonaigiki Gansai ({{jalink|January 10th|Q2243}}, {{jalink|May 10th|Q2557}}, {{jalink|September 10th|Q2853}}) * Spring Festival [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/event/index.html Kamigamo Shrine Festivals and Events] ({{jalink|April 10th|Q2502}}) ** Parishioners will be dedicating flower arrangements and praying throughout the day in the precincts. At the time of the prayer, ``Sato Kagura'' (Champon Kagura), a registered intangible folk cultural property of Kyoto City, is dedicated. Because Ota Shrine is a shrine with a long lifespan, it is characterized by the fact that it is performed by elderly people and has little movement. The name ``Champon Kagura'' was born from the sound. * Autumn Festival ({{jalink|November 10th|Q2988}}) ** Prayers and Kagura performances are accepted during the day, and a fire festival is held in the evening. A chrysanthemum exhibition will be held at the temple grounds throughout the day. === Kakitsubata community in Otanosawa === [[Image:Ota-jinja Kakitsubata.jpg|thumb|190px|Ota-jinja Kakitsubata community (national {{jalink|natural monument|Q23790}})]] At ``Otanozawa'' next to the approach, approximately 25,000 {{jalink|Kakitsubata|Q159320}} plants grow wild on a site of approximately 2,000 square meters, and it is called ``''Otanosawa's Kakitsubata colony''''. This Otanosawa has been a famous place since the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}, and there is a legend that it was the motif of {{jalink|Korin Ogata|Q952938}}'s ``Kakitsubata'' [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/topics/2010/2010_kakitsubata.html Kamigamo Shrine 2010 Information on Ota Shrine "Kakitsubata"]. When it blooms from early to mid-May every year, it blooms in various shades of purple all over the stream, delighting the eyes of many tourists.Otanosawa is said to have been a swamp in ancient times, similar to {{jalink|Shindoike|Q11560138}}, and is said to be a vestige of the time when {{jalink|Kyoto Basin|Q10883263}} was once a lake. In 1939|Q18639}, it was designated as a national {{jalink|natural monument|Q23790}}, along with the Kakitsubata community. In {{jalink|Bunji|Q1194415}}6 ({{jalink|1190|Q19736}}), the famous editor of ``{{jalink|Senzai Wakashu|Q1143290}}'', {{jalink|Fujiwara Toshinari|Q934255}}, , he composes the following song, likening the scene of being dyed in purple to a simple love. ``Violet grass shines bright in May, best time to see it at Ota Shrine in Kita Ward'' Kyoto Shimbun, May 6, 2010]. {{Quotation|Kakitsubata of the Kamiyama (Koyama) and Ota's swamp The flowers gleam in color
''The meaning of the song: Is the love affair that is deeply requested to the Kakitsubata of Ota Shrine near Kamiyama (the place where Kamobetsu Rai no Mikoto descended) as earnest and beautiful as the color of the Kakitsubata? ''|{{jalink|Toshinari Fujiwara|Q934255}}}} === Legend of full pond and dry pond === It is said that when praying for rain, if you change the water in Ota-no-ike (another name for Ota-no-sawa), it will rain, and during long rains, if you change the water in Jinguji-no-ike (the pond that used to be part of Jingu-ji Temple at Kamigamo Shrine), the rain will stop. Likened to {{jalink|Manju Island/Kanju Island|Q11563922|Kanju/Manju}}, the pond in Ota was called Manchi, and the pond at Jinguji Temple was called Hanike.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url= https://rmda.kulib.kyoto-u.ac.jp/item/rb00006109#?c=0&m=0&s=0&cv=0&r=0&xywh=-1144%2C-115%2C5358%2C2275 |title=Kyoto University Digital Archive Rare Document Images - Kamo Famous Places Stories|year=2002 |accessdate=2023-10-04|publisher={{jalink|Kyoto University|Q336264|Kyoto University Library}}}}. In the past, {{jalink|Eight Great Dragon Kings|Q11390736}} was enshrined in the pond, and HachiikeA collective name for {{jalink|Kibune Shrine|Q276779|Kibune}} Ryuo Falls, Nonaka Shimizu, Funasashi, Jinguji Pond (dry pond), Ota Pond (full pond), Kurojaike Pond, Shinjaike Pond, and {{jalink|Shindoike Pond|Q11560138|Midoike}}, where the Eight Great Dragonlords were solicited. (From "Kamo Meisho Monogatari" held by Kyoto University Library) was counted as one of the === Ota's path === At the rear of the shrine grounds, there is a walking path with a total length of approximately 750 meters called ``Ota no Komichi''. This walking path was developed in {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} in 2017 ({{jalink|2005|Q2019}}) by the Kamigamo Neighborhood Association, the Kamigamo Town Development Committee, and other members of local residents. {{PDFlink|[https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/sogo/cmsfiles/contents/0000035/35299/sono3-3-2.pdf Community Activities Support Guide Efforts of Kita Ward Kamigamo School District Community development with local "pride"]}}.There is an unpaved but well-maintained mountain path that runs up the small mountain behind the shrine. There are {{jalink|ropes|Q31029}} on both sides of the walking path, and there are {{jalink|signposts|Q1211272}} at several places along the way, so you can stroll without getting lost. {{jalink|Azalea|Azalea|Q189393|Mountain azalea}}, which is said to have been loved by Kitaoji Rosanjin|Q7367324}}, greets you as you walk along the mountain path. On good days, you can see {{jalink|Kyoto Tower|Q1201304}} and {{jalink|Fushimi Castle|Q1196273|Fushimi Momoyama Castle}}. By going through the cedar forest and following the walking path, you will reach the Okamoto Exit (east side climbing entrance, Kamigamo Okahoncho area) [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/kita/page/0000056124.html Kyoto City Information Center Kita Ward Office Our Town Kita Ward - Kamigamo School District News][https://www.city.kyoto.jp/kita/map/spot2.html#oota_komiti Kita Ward Spot Guide - Kyoto City Kita Ward Walking Map Ota Path]. The guide board for ``Ota no Komichi'' is written by the chief priest of Kamigamo Shrine on the Ota Shrine side and by the Mayor of Kyoto City's Kita Ward on the Okamoto Exit side. Image:Otanokomichi1.JPG|Entrance to Ota Shrine side Image:Otanokomichi2.JPG|Ota's small mountain path Image:Otanokomichi3.JPG|The view from the observation pointImage:Otanokomichi4.JPG|Okamoto exit side entrance === Cultural assets === === National Natural Monument === * Otanosawa's Kakitsubata community - Designated on September 7, 1945 {{Nationally designated cultural properties database|401|1694|Otanosawa's Kakitsubata community}}. === Kyoto City Registered Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Ota Shrine Shrine Goddess Music - Registered on May 1, 1986 [http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005866.html Kyoto City Designated and Registered Cultural Properties - Intangible Folk Cultural Properties - Kita Ward] (Kyoto City website "Kyoto City Information Center"). . === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group=†}} === Source === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|Japanese Izumitani|chapter=Ota Shrine|year=1979|title=Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report Volume 1|publisher={{jalink|Kogakkan University|Q1797584}}Publishing Department|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Ota Shrine (Shikiuchi Shrine)|1979}}}} * {{Cite book|Japanese book|author=Mitsugi Kennai|chapter=The history of Setsusesha|year=2003|title=Kamigamo Shrine|publisher={{jalink|Kamikamo Shrine|Q11448819}}|isbn=4311407203|ref={{Harvid|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Company)|2003}}}} === Related items === {{Commonscat|Ota-jinja (Kyoto)}} * {{jalink|Kamobetsu Rai Shrine|Q700448}} * {{ill|Q700448|lt=Q700448|WD=Q700448}} == image of entrance (P9721) == * Ota-jinja1.JPG {{commons category|Category:Ota-jinja (Kyoto)}} [[JA:大田神社 (京都市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11437955}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Ota Shrine |Image = [[File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) haiden.JPG|280px]]
House of worship |Location = {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}}{{ill|lt=Kyoto City|Q34600|WD=Q34600}}{{ill|lt=Kita Ward (Kyoto City)|Q1154970|WD=Q1154970|lt=Kita Ward}}Kamigamo Motoyama 340 |Location = {{Coord|35|03|35.4|N|135|45|30.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ota Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Amenouzume|Q462803|WD=Q462803|lt=Tenden Onme no Mikoto}} |Divinity = |Shaku = {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (small)
{{ill|lt=Kamobetsu Raijinja|Q700448|WD=Q700448}}Kyotogaisetsusha |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}{{ill|lt=cypress bark roof|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477}} |Alternative name = Ontasha |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=April 10th|Q2502|WD=Q2502}}・{{ill|lt=November 10th|Q2988|WD=Q2988}}{{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}} |Shinto ritual = }} [[File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) torii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Torii]]'''Ota Shrine'' (Ota Jinja) is {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in the Kamigamo main temple. {{ill||lt=Shikinai Shrine|UNKNOWN}}, currently outside the precincts of {{ill|lt=Kamobetsu Rai Shrine|Q700448|WD=Q700448}} (Kamigamo Shrine) {{ill|lt=Setsusha|Q135009904|WD=Q135009904}} (Third Setsusha). In ancient times, it was also called ``Ontasha''. It is located approximately 500 meters east of Kamigamo Shrine. === Enshrined God === The following deities are enshrined: {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. * '''{{ill|lt=Ame no Uzume|Q462803|WD=Q462803|lt=Ame no Uzume}}''' (Ameno Uzume Mikoto) It is said to be the oldest shrine in Kamo, and is believed to have longevity {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. === History === Founding date is unknown. It is said that it was worshiped by the indigenous people before {{ill|lt=Kamo Prefectural Lord|Q2975287|WD=Q2975287|lt=Kamo Prefectural Lord}} (Kamo Agatanushi) immigrated to this area, but it is not clear {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. {{ill|lt=extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended}} In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}) nk|Yamashiro Province|Q749276}}{{ill|lt=Atago District|Q10981345|WD=Q10981345}}, it is written as ``Ota Shrine'' (or ``Ota Shrine'' in some manuscripts), and is listed in {{ill||lt=Shikiuchi|UNKNOWN}} {{Sfn|Ota Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1979}}. === Precincts === === Shrine building === [[File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) honden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|main hall]] Both the main shrine and worship hall were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913}}5 ({{ill|lt=1628|Q6840|WD=Q6840}}) {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. The main shrine is an Ichigen shrine {{ill|lt=Nagazuzukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}, and the roof is {{ill|lt=cypress bark|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477}}{{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. The worship hall is an old style called a ``warihaiden'' (a worship hall with an atrium in the center that can be passed through), and the roof is made of cypress bark like the main shrine {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. In the company journal, it appears that the Ota oratory "toppled over" in March of {{ill|lt=Tenju (Japan)|Q1145023|WD=Q1145023|lt=Tenju}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1377|Q6289|WD=Q6289}}), and it appears that it was built before that {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. === Snake Pillow === [[Image:Ota-jinja3.JPG|thumb|150px|Snake pillow (lower center stone)]] A small stone peeks out from the water below the right side of the stone bridge in front of the torii gate. This stone is called ``''Snake's Pillow'' or ``Amaishi'' and is said to have been used as a pillow by a snake. Snakes were thought to be creatures that brought rain, and it was believed that if you went to the pillow where this snake was located, you could pray for rain. The ritual is performed by hitting this pillow stone with agricultural tools (iron tools). By doing this, the snake that is hit by the pillow gets angry and starts raining ``The Snake Pillow of Ota Shrine'' (Talk with Rocks 141) Kyoto Shimbun July 8, 1999. === Setsusha === * Shirahige Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{ill||lt=Sarutahiko no Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} * Hyakudaifusha - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Funatama-kami|Q11614362|WD=Q11614362|lt=Funatama-kami}} (Funatama no Kami) * Chinjusha - Deities enshrined: {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi no kami}} (Ookuninushi no kami), {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahiko no kami}} (Sukunahiko no kami) * Fukutokusha - Enshrined deity: Fukutokujin All of the above four shrines are positioned as outer shrines of Kamigamo Shrine {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=57-59}}. File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) Shirahige-sha.JPG|Shirahige-sha File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) Hyakudayu-sha.JPG|Hyakudayu-sha File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) Chinju-sha.JPG|Chinjusha === Festival === * Gonaigiki Gansai ({{ill|lt=January 10th|Q2243|WD=Q2243}}, {{ill|lt=May 10th|Q2557|WD=Q2557}}, {{ill|lt=September 10th|Q2853|WD=Q2853}}) * Spring Festival [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/event/index.html Kamigamo Shrine Festivals and Events] ({{ill|lt=April 10th|Q2502|WD=Q2502}}) ** Parishioners will be dedicating flower arrangements and praying throughout the day in the precincts. At the time of the prayer, ``Sato Kagura'' (Champon Kagura), a registered intangible folk cultural property of Kyoto City, is dedicated. Because Ota Shrine is a shrine with a long lifespan, it is characterized by the fact that it is performed by elderly people and has little movement. The name ``Champon Kagura'' was born from the sound. * Autumn Festival ({{ill|lt=November 10th|Q2988|WD=Q2988}}) ** Prayers and Kagura performances are accepted during the day, and a fire festival is held in the evening. A chrysanthemum exhibition will be held at the temple grounds throughout the day. === Kakitsubata community in Otanosawa === [[Image:Ota-jinja Kakitsubata.jpg|thumb|190px|Ota-jinja Kakitsubata community (national {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790}})]] At ``Otanozawa'' next to the approach, approximately 25,000 {{ill|lt=Kakitsubata|Q159320|WD=Q159320}} plants grow wild on a site of approximately 2,000 square meters, and it is called ``''Otanosawa's Kakitsubata colony''''. This Otanosawa has been a famous place since the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, and there is a legend that it was the motif of {{ill|lt=Korin Ogata|Q952938|WD=Q952938}}'s ``Kakitsubata'' [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/topics/2010/2010_kakitsubata.html Kamigamo Shrine 2010 Information on Ota Shrine "Kakitsubata"]. When it blooms from early to mid-May every year, it blooms in various shades of purple all over the stream, delighting the eyes of many tourists.Otanosawa is said to have been a swamp in ancient times, similar to {{ill|lt=Shindoike|Q11560138|WD=Q11560138}}, and is said to be a vestige of the time when {{ill|lt=Kyoto Basin|Q10883263|WD=Q10883263}} was once a lake. In 1939|Q18639}, it was designated as a national {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790}}, along with the Kakitsubata community. In {{ill|lt=Bunji|Q1194415|WD=Q1194415}}6 ({{ill|lt=1190|Q19736|WD=Q19736}}), the famous editor of ``{{ill|lt=Senzai Wakashu|Q1143290|WD=Q1143290}}'', {{ill|lt=Fujiwara Toshinari|Q934255|WD=Q934255}}, , he composes the following song, likening the scene of being dyed in purple to a simple love. ``Violet grass shines bright in May, best time to see it at Ota Shrine in Kita Ward'' Kyoto Shimbun, May 6, 2010]. {{Quotation|Kakitsubata of the Kamiyama (Koyama) and Ota's swamp The flowers gleam in color
''The meaning of the song: Is the love affair that is deeply requested to the Kakitsubata of Ota Shrine near Kamiyama (the place where Kamobetsu Rai no Mikoto descended) as earnest and beautiful as the color of the Kakitsubata? ''|{{ill|lt=Toshinari Fujiwara|Q934255|WD=Q934255}}}} === Legend of full pond and dry pond === It is said that when praying for rain, if you change the water in Ota-no-ike (another name for Ota-no-sawa), it will rain, and during long rains, if you change the water in Jinguji-no-ike (the pond that used to be part of Jingu-ji Temple at Kamigamo Shrine), the rain will stop. Likened to {{ill|lt=Manju Island/Kanju Island|Q11563922|WD=Q11563922|lt=Kanju/Manju}}, the pond in Ota was called Manchi, and the pond at Jinguji Temple was called Hanike.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url= https://rmda.kulib.kyoto-u.ac.jp/item/rb00006109#?c=0&m=0&s=0&cv=0&r=0&xywh=-1144%2C-115%2C5358%2C2275 |title=Kyoto University Digital Archive Rare Document Images - Kamo Famous Places Stories|year=2002 |accessdate=2023-10-04|publisher={{ill|lt=Kyoto University|Q336264|WD=Q336264|lt=Kyoto University Library}}}}. In the past, {{ill|lt=Eight Great Dragon Kings|Q11390736|WD=Q11390736}} was enshrined in the pond, and HachiikeA collective name for {{ill|lt=Kibune Shrine|Q276779|WD=Q276779|lt=Kibune}} Ryuo Falls, Nonaka Shimizu, Funasashi, Jinguji Pond (dry pond), Ota Pond (full pond), Kurojaike Pond, Shinjaike Pond, and {{ill|lt=Shindoike Pond|Q11560138|WD=Q11560138|lt=Midoike}}, where the Eight Great Dragonlords were solicited. (From "Kamo Meisho Monogatari" held by Kyoto University Library) was counted as one of the === Ota's path === At the rear of the shrine grounds, there is a walking path with a total length of approximately 750 meters called ``Ota no Komichi''. This walking path was developed in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} in 2017 ({{ill|lt=2005|Q2019|WD=Q2019}}) by the Kamigamo Neighborhood Association, the Kamigamo Town Development Committee, and other members of local residents. {{PDFlink|[https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/sogo/cmsfiles/contents/0000035/35299/sono3-3-2.pdf Community Activities Support Guide Efforts of Kita Ward Kamigamo School District Community development with local "pride"]}}.There is an unpaved but well-maintained mountain path that runs up the small mountain behind the shrine. There are {{ill|lt=ropes|Q31029|WD=Q31029}} on both sides of the walking path, and there are {{ill|lt=signposts|Q1211272|WD=Q1211272}} at several places along the way, so you can stroll without getting lost. {{ill|lt=Azalea|Azalea|WD=Azalea|lt=Q189393}}, which is said to have been loved by Kitaoji Rosanjin|Q7367324}}, greets you as you walk along the mountain path. On good days, you can see {{ill|lt=Kyoto Tower|Q1201304|WD=Q1201304}} and {{ill|lt=Fushimi Castle|Q1196273|WD=Q1196273|lt=Fushimi Momoyama Castle}}. By going through the cedar forest and following the walking path, you will reach the Okamoto Exit (east side climbing entrance, Kamigamo Okahoncho area) [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/kita/page/0000056124.html Kyoto City Information Center Kita Ward Office Our Town Kita Ward - Kamigamo School District News][https://www.city.kyoto.jp/kita/map/spot2.html#oota_komiti Kita Ward Spot Guide - Kyoto City Kita Ward Walking Map Ota Path]. The guide board for ``Ota no Komichi'' is written by the chief priest of Kamigamo Shrine on the Ota Shrine side and by the Mayor of Kyoto City's Kita Ward on the Okamoto Exit side. Image:Otanokomichi1.JPG|Entrance to Ota Shrine side Image:Otanokomichi2.JPG|Ota's small mountain path Image:Otanokomichi3.JPG|The view from the observation pointImage:Otanokomichi4.JPG|Okamoto exit side entrance === Cultural assets === === National Natural Monument === * Otanosawa's Kakitsubata community - Designated on September 7, 1945 {{Nationally designated cultural properties database|401|1694|Otanosawa's Kakitsubata community}}. === Kyoto City Registered Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Ota Shrine Shrine Goddess Music - Registered on May 1, 1986 [http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005866.html Kyoto City Designated and Registered Cultural Properties - Intangible Folk Cultural Properties - Kita Ward] (Kyoto City website "Kyoto City Information Center"). . === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group=†}} === Source === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|Japanese {{ill|Q700448|lt=Q700448|WD=Q700448}} == image of entrance (P9721) == * Ota-jinja1.JPG {{commons category|Category:Ota-jinja (Kyoto)}} [[JA:大田神社 (京都市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11437955}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Ota Shrine |Image = [[File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) haiden.JPG|280px]]
House of worship |Location = {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}}{{ill|lt=Kyoto City|Q34600|WD=Q34600}}{{ill|lt=Kita Ward (Kyoto City)|Q1154970|WD=Q1154970|lt=Kita Ward}}Kamigamo Motoyama 340 |Location = {{Coord|35|03|35.4|N|135|45|30.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ota Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Amenouzume|Q462803|WD=Q462803|lt=Tenden Onme no Mikoto}} |Divinity = |Shaku = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (small)
{{ill|lt=Kamobetsu Raijinja|Q700448|WD=Q700448}}Kyotogaisetsusha |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}{{ill|lt=cypress bark roof|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477}} |Alternative name = Ontasha |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=April 10th|Q2502|WD=Q2502}}・{{ill|lt=November 10th|Q2988|WD=Q2988}}{{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}} |Shinto ritual = }} [[File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) torii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Torii]]'''Ota Shrine'' (Ota Jinja) is {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in the Kamigamo main temple. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai Shrine|UNKNOWN}}, currently outside the precincts of {{ill|lt=Kamobetsu Rai Shrine|Q700448|WD=Q700448}} (Kamigamo Shrine) {{ill|lt=Setsusha|Q135009904|WD=Q135009904}} (Third Setsusha). In ancient times, it was also called ``Ontasha''. It is located approximately 500 meters east of Kamigamo Shrine. === Enshrined God === The following deities are enshrined: {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. * '''{{ill|lt=Ame no Uzume|Q462803|WD=Q462803|lt=Ame no Uzume}}''' (Ameno Uzume Mikoto) It is said to be the oldest shrine in Kamo, and is believed to have longevity {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. === History === Founding date is unknown. It is said that it was worshiped by the indigenous people before {{ill|lt=Kamo Prefectural Lord|Q2975287|WD=Q2975287|lt=Kamo Prefectural Lord}} (Kamo Agatanushi) immigrated to this area, but it is not clear {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. {{ill|lt=extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended}} In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}) nk|Yamashiro Province|Q749276}}{{ill|lt=Atago District|Q10981345|WD=Q10981345}}, it is written as ``Ota Shrine'' (or ``Ota Shrine'' in some manuscripts), and is listed in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikiuchi|UNKNOWN}} {{Sfn|Ota Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1979}}. === Precincts === === Shrine building === [[File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) honden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|main hall]] Both the main shrine and worship hall were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913}}5 ({{ill|lt=1628|Q6840|WD=Q6840}}) {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. The main shrine is an Ichigen shrine {{ill|lt=Nagazuzukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}, and the roof is {{ill|lt=cypress bark|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477}}{{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. The worship hall is an old style called a ``warihaiden'' (a worship hall with an atrium in the center that can be passed through), and the roof is made of cypress bark like the main shrine {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. In the company journal, it appears that the Ota oratory "toppled over" in March of {{ill|lt=Tenju (Japan)|Q1145023|WD=Q1145023|lt=Tenju}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1377|Q6289|WD=Q6289}}), and it appears that it was built before that {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. === Snake Pillow === [[Image:Ota-jinja3.JPG|thumb|150px|Snake pillow (lower center stone)]] A small stone peeks out from the water below the right side of the stone bridge in front of the torii gate. This stone is called ``''Snake's Pillow'' or ``Amaishi'' and is said to have been used as a pillow by a snake. Snakes were thought to be creatures that brought rain, and it was believed that if you went to the pillow where this snake was located, you could pray for rain. The ritual is performed by hitting this pillow stone with agricultural tools (iron tools). By doing this, the snake that is hit by the pillow gets angry and starts raining ``The Snake Pillow of Ota Shrine'' (Talk with Rocks 141) Kyoto Shimbun July 8, 1999. === Setsusha === * Shirahige Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sarutahiko no Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} * Hyakudaifusha - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Funatama-kami|Q11614362|WD=Q11614362|lt=Funatama-kami}} (Funatama no Kami) * Chinjusha - Deities enshrined: {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi no kami}} (Ookuninushi no kami), {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahiko no kami}} (Sukunahiko no kami) * Fukutokusha - Enshrined deity: Fukutokujin All of the above four shrines are positioned as outer shrines of Kamigamo Shrine {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=57-59}}. File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) Shirahige-sha.JPG|Shirahige-sha File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) Hyakudayu-sha.JPG|Hyakudayu-sha File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) Chinju-sha.JPG|Chinjusha === Festival === * Gonaigiki Gansai ({{ill|lt=January 10th|Q2243|WD=Q2243}}, {{ill|lt=May 10th|Q2557|WD=Q2557}}, {{ill|lt=September 10th|Q2853|WD=Q2853}}) * Spring Festival [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/event/index.html Kamigamo Shrine Festivals and Events] ({{ill|lt=April 10th|Q2502|WD=Q2502}}) ** Parishioners will be dedicating flower arrangements and praying throughout the day in the precincts. At the time of the prayer, ``Sato Kagura'' (Champon Kagura), a registered intangible folk cultural property of Kyoto City, is dedicated. Because Ota Shrine is a shrine with a long lifespan, it is characterized by the fact that it is performed by elderly people and has little movement. The name ``Champon Kagura'' was born from the sound. * Autumn Festival ({{ill|lt=November 10th|Q2988|WD=Q2988}}) ** Prayers and Kagura performances are accepted during the day, and a fire festival is held in the evening. A chrysanthemum exhibition will be held at the temple grounds throughout the day. === Kakitsubata community in Otanosawa === [[Image:Ota-jinja Kakitsubata.jpg|thumb|190px|Ota-jinja Kakitsubata community (national {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790}})]] At ``Otanozawa'' next to the approach, approximately 25,000 {{ill|lt=Kakitsubata|Q159320|WD=Q159320}} plants grow wild on a site of approximately 2,000 square meters, and it is called ``''Otanosawa's Kakitsubata colony''''. This Otanosawa has been a famous place since the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, and there is a legend that it was the motif of {{ill|lt=Korin Ogata|Q952938|WD=Q952938}}'s ``Kakitsubata'' [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/topics/2010/2010_kakitsubata.html Kamigamo Shrine 2010 Information on Ota Shrine "Kakitsubata"]. When it blooms from early to mid-May every year, it blooms in various shades of purple all over the stream, delighting the eyes of many tourists.Otanosawa is said to have been a swamp in ancient times, similar to {{ill|lt=Shindoike|Q11560138|WD=Q11560138}}, and is said to be a vestige of the time when {{ill|lt=Kyoto Basin|Q10883263|WD=Q10883263}} was once a lake. In 1939|Q18639}, it was designated as a national {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790}}, along with the Kakitsubata community. In {{ill|lt=Bunji|Q1194415|WD=Q1194415}}6 ({{ill|lt=1190|Q19736|WD=Q19736}}), the famous editor of ``{{ill|lt=Senzai Wakashu|Q1143290|WD=Q1143290}}'', {{ill|lt=Fujiwara Toshinari|Q934255|WD=Q934255}}, , he composes the following song, likening the scene of being dyed in purple to a simple love. ``Violet grass shines bright in May, best time to see it at Ota Shrine in Kita Ward'' Kyoto Shimbun, May 6, 2010]. {{Quotation|Kakitsubata of the Kamiyama (Koyama) and Ota's swamp The flowers gleam in color
''The meaning of the song: Is the love affair that is deeply requested to the Kakitsubata of Ota Shrine near Kamiyama (the place where Kamobetsu Rai no Mikoto descended) as earnest and beautiful as the color of the Kakitsubata? ''|{{ill|lt=Toshinari Fujiwara|Q934255|WD=Q934255}}}} === Legend of full pond and dry pond === It is said that when praying for rain, if you change the water in Ota-no-ike (another name for Ota-no-sawa), it will rain, and during long rains, if you change the water in Jinguji-no-ike (the pond that used to be part of Jingu-ji Temple at Kamigamo Shrine), the rain will stop. Likened to {{ill|lt=Manju Island/Kanju Island|Q11563922|WD=Q11563922|lt=Kanju/Manju}}, the pond in Ota was called Manchi, and the pond at Jinguji Temple was called Hanike.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url= https://rmda.kulib.kyoto-u.ac.jp/item/rb00006109#?c=0&m=0&s=0&cv=0&r=0&xywh=-1144%2C-115%2C5358%2C2275 |title=Kyoto University Digital Archive Rare Document Images - Kamo Famous Places Stories|year=2002 |accessdate=2023-10-04|publisher={{ill|lt=Kyoto University|Q336264|WD=Q336264|lt=Kyoto University Library}}}}. In the past, {{ill|lt=Eight Great Dragon Kings|Q11390736|WD=Q11390736}} was enshrined in the pond, and HachiikeA collective name for {{ill|lt=Kibune Shrine|Q276779|WD=Q276779|lt=Kibune}} Ryuo Falls, Nonaka Shimizu, Funasashi, Jinguji Pond (dry pond), Ota Pond (full pond), Kurojaike Pond, Shinjaike Pond, and {{ill|lt=Shindoike Pond|Q11560138|WD=Q11560138|lt=Midoike}}, where the Eight Great Dragonlords were solicited. (From "Kamo Meisho Monogatari" held by Kyoto University Library) was counted as one of the === Ota's path === At the rear of the shrine grounds, there is a walking path with a total length of approximately 750 meters called ``Ota no Komichi''. This walking path was developed in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} in 2017 ({{ill|lt=2005|Q2019|WD=Q2019}}) by the Kamigamo Neighborhood Association, the Kamigamo Town Development Committee, and other members of local residents. {{PDFlink|[https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/sogo/cmsfiles/contents/0000035/35299/sono3-3-2.pdf Community Activities Support Guide Efforts of Kita Ward Kamigamo School District Community development with local "pride"]}}.There is an unpaved but well-maintained mountain path that runs up the small mountain behind the shrine. There are {{ill|lt=ropes|Q31029|WD=Q31029}} on both sides of the walking path, and there are {{ill|lt=signposts|Q1211272|WD=Q1211272}} at several places along the way, so you can stroll without getting lost. {{ill|lt=Azalea|Azalea|WD=Azalea|lt=Q189393}}, which is said to have been loved by Kitaoji Rosanjin|Q7367324}}, greets you as you walk along the mountain path. On good days, you can see {{ill|lt=Kyoto Tower|Q1201304|WD=Q1201304}} and {{ill|lt=Fushimi Castle|Q1196273|WD=Q1196273|lt=Fushimi Momoyama Castle}}. By going through the cedar forest and following the walking path, you will reach the Okamoto Exit (east side climbing entrance, Kamigamo Okahoncho area) [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/kita/page/0000056124.html Kyoto City Information Center Kita Ward Office Our Town Kita Ward - Kamigamo School District News][https://www.city.kyoto.jp/kita/map/spot2.html#oota_komiti Kita Ward Spot Guide - Kyoto City Kita Ward Walking Map Ota Path]. The guide board for ``Ota no Komichi'' is written by the chief priest of Kamigamo Shrine on the Ota Shrine side and by the Mayor of Kyoto City's Kita Ward on the Okamoto Exit side. Image:Otanokomichi1.JPG|Entrance to Ota Shrine side Image:Otanokomichi2.JPG|Ota's small mountain path Image:Otanokomichi3.JPG|The view from the observation pointImage:Otanokomichi4.JPG|Okamoto exit side entrance === Cultural assets === === National Natural Monument === * Otanosawa's Kakitsubata community - Designated on September 7, 1945 {{Nationally designated cultural properties database|401|1694|Otanosawa's Kakitsubata community}}. === Kyoto City Registered Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Ota Shrine Shrine Goddess Music - Registered on May 1, 1986 [http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005866.html Kyoto City Designated and Registered Cultural Properties - Intangible Folk Cultural Properties - Kita Ward] (Kyoto City website "Kyoto City Information Center"). . === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group=†}} === Source === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|Japanese Izumitani|chapter=Ota Shrine|year=1979|title=Shikinai Shrine Investigation Report Volume 1|publisher={{ill|lt=Kogakkan University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584}}Publishing == {{Shrine |Name = Ota Shrine |Image = [[File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) haiden.JPG|280px]]
House of worship |Location = {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}}{{ill|lt=Kyoto City|Q34600|WD=Q34600}}{{ill|lt=Kita Ward (Kyoto City)|Q1154970|WD=Q1154970|lt=Kita Ward}}Kamigamo Motoyama 340 |Location = {{Coord|35|03|35.4|N|135|45|30.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ota Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Amenouzume|Q462803|WD=Q462803|lt=Tenden Onme no Mikoto}} |Divinity = |Shaku = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} (small)
{{ill|lt=Kamobetsu Raijinja|Q700448|WD=Q700448}}Kyotogaisetsusha |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}{{ill|lt=cypress bark roof|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477}} |Alternative name = Ontasha |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=April 10th|Q2502|WD=Q2502}}・{{ill|lt=November 10th|Q2988|WD=Q2988}}{{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}} |Shinto ritual = }} [[File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) torii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Torii]]'''Ota Shrine'' (Ota Jinja) is {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in the Kamigamo main temple. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, currently outside the precincts of {{ill|lt=Kamobetsu Rai Shrine|Q700448|WD=Q700448}} (Kamigamo Shrine) {{ill|lt=Setsusha|Q135009904|WD=Q135009904}} (Third Setsusha). In ancient times, it was also called ``Ontasha''. It is located approximately 500 meters east of Kamigamo Shrine. === Enshrined God === The following deities are enshrined: {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. * '''{{ill|lt=Ame no Uzume|Q462803|WD=Q462803|lt=Ame no Uzume}}''' (Ameno Uzume Mikoto) It is said to be the oldest shrine in Kamo, and is believed to have longevity {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. === History === Founding date is unknown. It is said that it was worshiped by the indigenous people before {{ill|lt=Kamo Prefectural Lord|Q2975287|WD=Q2975287|lt=Kamo Prefectural Lord}} (Kamo Agatanushi) immigrated to this area, but it is not clear {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. {{ill|lt=extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended}} In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}) nk|Yamashiro Province|Q749276}}{{ill|lt=Atago District|Q10981345|WD=Q10981345}}, it is written as ``Ota Shrine'' (or ``Ota Shrine'' in some manuscripts), and is listed in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikiuchi|1=UNKNOWN}} {{Sfn|Ota Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1979}}. === Precincts === === Shrine building === [[File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) honden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|main hall]] Both the main shrine and worship hall were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913}}5 ({{ill|lt=1628|Q6840|WD=Q6840}}) {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. The main shrine is an Ichigen shrine {{ill|lt=Nagazuzukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}, and the roof is {{ill|lt=cypress bark|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477}}{{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. The worship hall is an old style called a ``warihaiden'' (a worship hall with an atrium in the center that can be passed through), and the roof is made of cypress bark like the main shrine {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. In the company journal, it appears that the Ota oratory "toppled over" in March of {{ill|lt=Tenju (Japan)|Q1145023|WD=Q1145023|lt=Tenju}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1377|Q6289|WD=Q6289}}), and it appears that it was built before that {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. === Snake Pillow === [[Image:Ota-jinja3.JPG|thumb|150px|Snake pillow (lower center stone)]] A small stone peeks out from the water below the right side of the stone bridge in front of the torii gate. This stone is called ``''Snake's Pillow'' or ``Amaishi'' and is said to have been used as a pillow by a snake. Snakes were thought to be creatures that brought rain, and it was believed that if you went to the pillow where this snake was located, you could pray for rain. The ritual is performed by hitting this pillow stone with agricultural tools (iron tools). By doing this, the snake that is hit by the pillow gets angry and starts raining ``The Snake Pillow of Ota Shrine'' (Talk with Rocks 141) Kyoto Shimbun July 8, 1999. === Setsusha === * Shirahige Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sarutahiko no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} * Hyakudaifusha - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Funatama-kami|Q11614362|WD=Q11614362|lt=Funatama-kami}} (Funatama no Kami) * Chinjusha - Deities enshrined: {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi no kami}} (Ookuninushi no kami), {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahiko no kami}} (Sukunahiko no kami) * Fukutokusha - Enshrined deity: Fukutokujin All of the above four shrines are positioned as outer shrines of Kamigamo Shrine {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=57-59}}. File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) Shirahige-sha.JPG|Shirahige-sha File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) Hyakudayu-sha.JPG|Hyakudayu-sha File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) Chinju-sha.JPG|Chinjusha === Festival === * Gonaigiki Gansai ({{ill|lt=January 10th|Q2243|WD=Q2243}}, {{ill|lt=May 10th|Q2557|WD=Q2557}}, {{ill|lt=September 10th|Q2853|WD=Q2853}}) * Spring Festival [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/event/index.html Kamigamo Shrine Festivals and Events] ({{ill|lt=April 10th|Q2502|WD=Q2502}}) ** Parishioners will be dedicating flower arrangements and praying throughout the day in the precincts. At the time of the prayer, ``Sato Kagura'' (Champon Kagura), a registered intangible folk cultural property of Kyoto City, is dedicated. Because Ota Shrine is a shrine with a long lifespan, it is characterized by the fact that it is performed by elderly people and has little movement. The name ``Champon Kagura'' was born from the sound. * Autumn Festival ({{ill|lt=November 10th|Q2988|WD=Q2988}}) ** Prayers and Kagura performances are accepted during the day, and a fire festival is held in the evening. A chrysanthemum exhibition will be held at the temple grounds throughout the day. === Kakitsubata community in Otanosawa === [[Image:Ota-jinja Kakitsubata.jpg|thumb|190px|Ota-jinja Kakitsubata community (national {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790}})]] At ``Otanozawa'' next to the approach, approximately 25,000 {{ill|lt=Kakitsubata|Q159320|WD=Q159320}} plants grow wild on a site of approximately 2,000 square meters, and it is called ``''Otanosawa's Kakitsubata colony''''. This Otanosawa has been a famous place since the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, and there is a legend that it was the motif of {{ill|lt=Korin Ogata|Q952938|WD=Q952938}}'s ``Kakitsubata'' [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/topics/2010/2010_kakitsubata.html Kamigamo Shrine 2010 Information on Ota Shrine "Kakitsubata"]. When it blooms from early to mid-May every year, it blooms in various shades of purple all over the stream, delighting the eyes of many tourists.Otanosawa is said to have been a swamp in ancient times, similar to {{ill|lt=Shindoike|Q11560138|WD=Q11560138}}, and is said to be a vestige of the time when {{ill|lt=Kyoto Basin|Q10883263|WD=Q10883263}} was once a lake. In 1939|Q18639}, it was designated as a national {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790}}, along with the Kakitsubata community. In {{ill|lt=Bunji|Q1194415|WD=Q1194415}}6 ({{ill|lt=1190|Q19736|WD=Q19736}}), the famous editor of ``{{ill|lt=Senzai Wakashu|Q1143290|WD=Q1143290}}'', {{ill|lt=Fujiwara Toshinari|Q934255|WD=Q934255}}, , he composes the following song, likening the scene of being dyed in purple to a simple love. ``Violet grass shines bright in May, best time to see it at Ota Shrine in Kita Ward'' Kyoto Shimbun, May 6, 2010]. {{Quotation|Kakitsubata of the Kamiyama (Koyama) and Ota's swamp The flowers gleam in color
''The meaning of the song: Is the love affair that is deeply requested to the Kakitsubata of Ota Shrine near Kamiyama (the place where Kamobetsu Rai no Mikoto descended) as earnest and beautiful as the color of the Kakitsubata? ''|{{ill|lt=Toshinari Fujiwara|Q934255|WD=Q934255}}}} === Legend of full pond and dry pond === It is said that when praying for rain, if you change the water in Ota-no-ike (another name for Ota-no-sawa), it will rain, and during long rains, if you change the water in Jinguji-no-ike (the pond that used to be part of Jingu-ji Temple at Kamigamo Shrine), the rain will stop. Likened to {{ill|lt=Manju Island/Kanju Island|Q11563922|WD=Q11563922|lt=Kanju/Manju}}, the pond in Ota was called Manchi, and the pond at Jinguji Temple was called Hanike.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url= https://rmda.kulib.kyoto-u.ac.jp/item/rb00006109#?c=0&m=0&s=0&cv=0&r=0&xywh=-1144%2C-115%2C5358%2C2275 |title=Kyoto University Digital Archive Rare Document Images - Kamo Famous Places Stories|year=2002 |accessdate=2023-10-04|publisher={{ill|lt=Kyoto University|Q336264|WD=Q336264|lt=Kyoto University Library}}}}. In the past, {{ill|lt=Eight Great Dragon Kings|Q11390736|WD=Q11390736}} was enshrined in the pond, and HachiikeA collective name for {{ill|lt=Kibune Shrine|Q276779|WD=Q276779|lt=Kibune}} Ryuo Falls, Nonaka Shimizu, Funasashi, Jinguji Pond (dry pond), Ota Pond (full pond), Kurojaike Pond, Shinjaike Pond, and {{ill|lt=Shindoike Pond|Q11560138|WD=Q11560138|lt=Midoike}}, where the Eight Great Dragonlords were solicited. (From "Kamo Meisho Monogatari" held by Kyoto University Library) was counted as one of the === Ota's path === At the rear of the shrine grounds, there is a walking path with a total length of approximately 750 meters called ``Ota no Komichi''. This walking path was developed in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} in 2017 ({{ill|lt=2005|Q2019|WD=Q2019}}) by the Kamigamo Neighborhood Association, the Kamigamo Town Development Committee, and other members of local residents. {{PDFlink|[https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/sogo/cmsfiles/contents/0000035/35299/sono3-3-2.pdf Community Activities Support Guide Efforts of Kita Ward Kamigamo School District Community development with local "pride"]}}.There is an unpaved but well-maintained mountain path that runs up the small mountain behind the shrine. There are {{ill|lt=ropes|Q31029|WD=Q31029}} on both sides of the walking path, and there are {{ill|lt=signposts|Q1211272|WD=Q1211272}} at several places along the way, so you can stroll without getting lost. {{ill|lt=Azalea|Azalea|WD=Azalea|lt=Q189393}}, which is said to have been loved by Kitaoji Rosanjin|Q7367324}}, greets you as you walk along the mountain path. On good days, you can see {{ill|lt=Kyoto Tower|Q1201304|WD=Q1201304}} and {{ill|lt=Fushimi Castle|Q1196273|WD=Q1196273|lt=Fushimi Momoyama Castle}}. By going through the cedar forest and following the walking path, you will reach the Okamoto Exit (east side climbing entrance, Kamigamo Okahoncho area) [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/kita/page/0000056124.html Kyoto City Information Center Kita Ward Office Our Town Kita Ward - Kamigamo School District News][https://www.city.kyoto.jp/kita/map/spot2.html#oota_komiti Kita Ward Spot Guide - Kyoto City Kita Ward Walking Map Ota Path]. The guide board for ``Ota no Komichi'' is written by the chief priest of Kamigamo Shrine on the Ota Shrine side and by the Mayor of Kyoto City's Kita Ward on the Okamoto Exit side. {{ill|Q700448|lt=Q700448|WD=Q700448}} == image of entrance (P9721) == * Ota-jinja1.JPG {{commons category|Category:Ota-jinja (Kyoto)}} [[JA:大田神社 (京都市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11437955}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Ota Shrine |Image = [[File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) haiden.JPG|280px]]
House of worship |Location = {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}}{{ill|lt=Kyoto City|Q34600|WD=Q34600}}{{ill|lt=Kita Ward (Kyoto City)|Q1154970|WD=Q1154970|lt=Kita Ward}}Kamigamo Motoyama 340 |Location = {{Coord|35|03|35.4|N|135|45|30.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ota Shrine}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|lt=Amenouzume|Q462803|WD=Q462803|lt=Tenden Onme no Mikoto}} |Divinity = |Shaku = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
{{ill|lt=Kamobetsu Raijinja|Q700448|WD=Q700448}}Kyotogaisetsusha |Foundation = unknown |Main shrine = Ikkensha {{ill|lt=Ryuzo|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}{{ill|lt=cypress bark roof|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477}} |Alternative name = Ontasha |Fudasho, etc. = |Regular Festival = {{ill|lt=April 10th|Q2502|WD=Q2502}}・{{ill|lt=November 10th|Q2988|WD=Q2988}}{{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}} |Shinto ritual = }} [[File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) torii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Torii]]'''Ota Shrine'' (Ota Jinja) is {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} located in the Kamigamo main temple. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, currently outside the precincts of {{ill|lt=Kamobetsu Rai Shrine|Q700448|WD=Q700448}} (Kamigamo Shrine) {{ill|lt=Setsusha|Q135009904|WD=Q135009904}} (Third Setsusha). In ancient times, it was also called ``Ontasha''. It is located approximately 500 meters east of Kamigamo Shrine. === Enshrined God === The following deities are enshrined: {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. * '''{{ill|lt=Ame no Uzume|Q462803|WD=Q462803|lt=Ame no Uzume}}''' (Ameno Uzume Mikoto) It is said to be the oldest shrine in Kamo, and is believed to have longevity {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. === History === Founding date is unknown. It is said that it was worshiped by the indigenous people before {{ill|lt=Kamo Prefectural Lord|Q2975287|WD=Q2975287|lt=Kamo Prefectural Lord}} (Kamo Agatanushi) immigrated to this area, but it is not clear {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. {{ill|lt=extension (era name)|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274|lt=Extended}} In "{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}" established in 5 years ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}}) nk|Yamashiro Province|Q749276}}{{ill|lt=Atago District|Q10981345|WD=Q10981345}}, it is written as ``Ota Shrine'' (or ``Ota Shrine'' in some manuscripts), and is listed in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikiuchi|1=UNKNOWN}} {{Sfn|Ota Shrine (Shikiuchi)|1979}}. === Precincts === === Shrine building === [[File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) honden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|main hall]] Both the main shrine and worship hall were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Kanei|Q1192913|WD=Q1192913}}5 ({{ill|lt=1628|Q6840|WD=Q6840}}) {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. The main shrine is an Ichigen shrine {{ill|lt=Nagazuzukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}}, and the roof is {{ill|lt=cypress bark|Q90373477|WD=Q90373477}}{{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. The worship hall is an old style called a ``warihaiden'' (a worship hall with an atrium in the center that can be passed through), and the roof is made of cypress bark like the main shrine {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. In the company journal, it appears that the Ota oratory "toppled over" in March of {{ill|lt=Tenju (Japan)|Q1145023|WD=Q1145023|lt=Tenju}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=1377|Q6289|WD=Q6289}}), and it appears that it was built before that {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=45-46}}. === Snake Pillow === [[Image:Ota-jinja3.JPG|thumb|150px|Snake pillow (lower center stone)]] A small stone peeks out from the water below the right side of the stone bridge in front of the torii gate. This stone is called ``''Snake's Pillow'' or ``Amaishi'' and is said to have been used as a pillow by a snake. Snakes were thought to be creatures that brought rain, and it was believed that if you went to the pillow where this snake was located, you could pray for rain. The ritual is performed by hitting this pillow stone with agricultural tools (iron tools). By doing this, the snake that is hit by the pillow gets angry and starts raining ``The Snake Pillow of Ota Shrine'' (Talk with Rocks 141) Kyoto Shimbun July 8, 1999. === Setsusha === * Shirahige Shrine - Enshrined Deity: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sarutahiko no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} * Hyakudaifusha - Deity enshrined: {{ill|lt=Funatama-kami|Q11614362|WD=Q11614362|lt=Funatama-kami}} (Funatama no Kami) * Chinjusha - Deities enshrined: {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi no kami}} (Ookuninushi no kami), {{ill|lt=Sukunabikona|Q3275687|WD=Q3275687|lt=Sukunahiko no kami}} (Sukunahiko no kami) * Fukutokusha - Enshrined deity: Fukutokujin All of the above four shrines are positioned as outer shrines of Kamigamo Shrine {{Sfn|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Shrine)|2003|pp=57-59}}. File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) Shirahige-sha.JPG|Shirahige-sha File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) Hyakudayu-sha.JPG|Hyakudayu-sha File:Ota-jinja (Kyoto) Chinju-sha.JPG|Chinjusha === Festival === * Gonaigiki Gansai ({{ill|lt=January 10th|Q2243|WD=Q2243}}, {{ill|lt=May 10th|Q2557|WD=Q2557}}, {{ill|lt=September 10th|Q2853|WD=Q2853}}) * Spring Festival [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/event/index.html Kamigamo Shrine Festivals and Events] ({{ill|lt=April 10th|Q2502|WD=Q2502}}) ** Parishioners will be dedicating flower arrangements and praying throughout the day in the precincts. At the time of the prayer, ``Sato Kagura'' (Champon Kagura), a registered intangible folk cultural property of Kyoto City, is dedicated. Because Ota Shrine is a shrine with a long lifespan, it is characterized by the fact that it is performed by elderly people and has little movement. The name ``Champon Kagura'' was born from the sound. * Autumn Festival ({{ill|lt=November 10th|Q2988|WD=Q2988}}) ** Prayers and Kagura performances are accepted during the day, and a fire festival is held in the evening. A chrysanthemum exhibition will be held at the temple grounds throughout the day. === Kakitsubata community in Otanosawa === [[Image:Ota-jinja Kakitsubata.jpg|thumb|190px|Ota-jinja Kakitsubata community (national {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790}})]] At ``Otanozawa'' next to the approach, approximately 25,000 {{ill|lt=Kakitsubata|Q159320|WD=Q159320}} plants grow wild on a site of approximately 2,000 square meters, and it is called ``''Otanosawa's Kakitsubata colony''''. This Otanosawa has been a famous place since the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}, and there is a legend that it was the motif of {{ill|lt=Korin Ogata|Q952938|WD=Q952938}}'s ``Kakitsubata'' [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp/topics/2010/2010_kakitsubata.html Kamigamo Shrine 2010 Information on Ota Shrine "Kakitsubata"]. When it blooms from early to mid-May every year, it blooms in various shades of purple all over the stream, delighting the eyes of many tourists.Otanosawa is said to have been a swamp in ancient times, similar to {{ill|lt=Shindoike|Q11560138|WD=Q11560138}}, and is said to be a vestige of the time when {{ill|lt=Kyoto Basin|Q10883263|WD=Q10883263}} was once a lake. In 1939|Q18639}, it was designated as a national {{ill|lt=natural monument|Q23790|WD=Q23790}}, along with the Kakitsubata community. In {{ill|lt=Bunji|Q1194415|WD=Q1194415}}6 ({{ill|lt=1190|Q19736|WD=Q19736}}), the famous editor of ``{{ill|lt=Senzai Wakashu|Q1143290|WD=Q1143290}}'', {{ill|lt=Fujiwara Toshinari|Q934255|WD=Q934255}}, , he composes the following song, likening the scene of being dyed in purple to a simple love. ``Violet grass shines bright in May, best time to see it at Ota Shrine in Kita Ward'' Kyoto Shimbun, May 6, 2010]. {{Quotation|Kakitsubata of the Kamiyama (Koyama) and Ota's swamp The flowers gleam in color
''The meaning of the song: Is the love affair that is deeply requested to the Kakitsubata of Ota Shrine near Kamiyama (the place where Kamobetsu Rai no Mikoto descended) as earnest and beautiful as the color of the Kakitsubata? ''|{{ill|lt=Toshinari Fujiwara|Q934255|WD=Q934255}}}} === Legend of full pond and dry pond === It is said that when praying for rain, if you change the water in Ota-no-ike (another name for Ota-no-sawa), it will rain, and during long rains, if you change the water in Jinguji-no-ike (the pond that used to be part of Jingu-ji Temple at Kamigamo Shrine), the rain will stop. Likened to {{ill|lt=Manju Island/Kanju Island|Q11563922|WD=Q11563922|lt=Kanju/Manju}}, the pond in Ota was called Manchi, and the pond at Jinguji Temple was called Hanike.{{Cite web|Japanese book|url= https://rmda.kulib.kyoto-u.ac.jp/item/rb00006109#?c=0&m=0&s=0&cv=0&r=0&xywh=-1144%2C-115%2C5358%2C2275 |title=Kyoto University Digital Archive Rare Document Images - Kamo Famous Places Stories|year=2002 |accessdate=2023-10-04|publisher={{ill|lt=Kyoto University|Q336264|WD=Q336264|lt=Kyoto University Library}}}}. In the past, {{ill|lt=Eight Great Dragon Kings|Q11390736|WD=Q11390736}} was enshrined in the pond, and HachiikeA collective name for {{ill|lt=Kibune Shrine|Q276779|WD=Q276779|lt=Kibune}} Ryuo Falls, Nonaka Shimizu, Funasashi, Jinguji Pond (dry pond), Ota Pond (full pond), Kurojaike Pond, Shinjaike Pond, and {{ill|lt=Shindoike Pond|Q11560138|WD=Q11560138|lt=Midoike}}, where the Eight Great Dragonlords were solicited. (From "Kamo Meisho Monogatari" held by Kyoto University Library) was counted as one of the === Ota's path === At the rear of the shrine grounds, there is a walking path with a total length of approximately 750 meters called ``Ota no Komichi''. This walking path was developed in {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} in 2017 ({{ill|lt=2005|Q2019|WD=Q2019}}) by the Kamigamo Neighborhood Association, the Kamigamo Town Development Committee, and other members of local residents. {{PDFlink|[https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/sogo/cmsfiles/contents/0000035/35299/sono3-3-2.pdf Community Activities Support Guide Efforts of Kita Ward Kamigamo School District Community development with local "pride"]}}.There is an unpaved but well-maintained mountain path that runs up the small mountain behind the shrine. There are {{ill|lt=ropes|Q31029|WD=Q31029}} on both sides of the walking path, and there are {{ill|lt=signposts|Q1211272|WD=Q1211272}} at several places along the way, so you can stroll without getting lost. {{ill|lt=Azalea|Azalea|WD=Azalea|lt=Q189393}}, which is said to have been loved by Kitaoji Rosanjin|Q7367324}}, greets you as you walk along the mountain path. On good days, you can see {{ill|lt=Kyoto Tower|Q1201304|WD=Q1201304}} and {{ill|lt=Fushimi Castle|Q1196273|WD=Q1196273|lt=Fushimi Momoyama Castle}}. By going through the cedar forest and following the walking path, you will reach the Okamoto Exit (east side climbing entrance, Kamigamo Okahoncho area) [https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/kita/page/0000056124.html Kyoto City Information Center Kita Ward Office Our Town Kita Ward - Kamigamo School District News][https://www.city.kyoto.jp/kita/map/spot2.html#oota_komiti Kita Ward Spot Guide - Kyoto City Kita Ward Walking Map Ota Path]. The guide board for ``Ota no Komichi'' is written by the chief priest of Kamigamo Shrine on the Ota Shrine side and by the Mayor of Kyoto City's Kita Ward on the Okamoto Exit side. Image:Otanokomichi1.JPG|Entrance to Ota Shrine side Image:Otanokomichi2.JPG|Ota's small mountain path Image:Otanokomichi3.JPG|The view from the observation pointImage:Otanokomichi4.JPG|Okamoto exit side entrance === Cultural assets === === National Natural Monument === * Otanosawa's Kakitsubata community - Designated on September 7, 1945 {{Nationally designated cultural properties database|401|1694|Otanosawa's Kakitsubata community}}. === Kyoto City Registered Intangible Folk Cultural Property === * Ota Shrine Shrine Goddess Music - Registered on May 1, 1986 [http://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp/bunshi/page/0000005866.html Kyoto City Designated and Registered Cultural Properties - Intangible Folk Cultural Properties - Kita Ward] (Kyoto City website "Kyoto City Information Center"). . === Footnotes === {{Footnote help}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group=†}} === Source === {{Reflist|2}} === References === * Precinct information board * {{Cite book|Japanese Shrine|publisher={{ill|lt=Kamikamo Shrine|Q11448819|WD=Q11448819}}|isbn=4311407203|ref={{Harvid|Kamigamo Shrine (Student Company)|2003}}}} === Related items === {{Commonscat|Ota-jinja (Kyoto)}} *
